title: Scalability to Web Data
concept: Parallelization lets Transformers scale to billions of parameters trained on massive web corpora (BookCorpus, Wikipedia).

exercises.prompt: In computer science, what does "parallel processing" mean?

options.text: Doing many calculations at the exact same time.
Doing calculations one by one in a strict line.
Deleting half the words to save storage space.

exercises.explain: Transformers are designed to run calculations in parallel, meaning they do many computations at the same time instead of waiting for the previous step to finish.

cards.title: Reading the Entire Web
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how AI models like ChatGPT seem to know a little bit about everything? It is because they have read a massive portion of the public internet! But how do you teach an AI millions of books and web pages without waiting a thousand years? The secret is training in parallel.

cards.title: What is Parallelization?
cards.body: **Parallelization** is the ability to do many computations at the same time instead of one after another. Because Transformers look at all words in a sentence at once, they can split the massive training workload across thousands of computers simultaneously. This makes it possible to train them on gigantic collections of web data like Wikipedia and BookCorpus.

cards.title: The Copyist Team
cards.body: Imagine copying a 1,000-page book by hand. If you work alone, you write page by page, which takes forever. But if you tear the book into pages and give one page to 1,000 friends to copy at the same time, the job gets done in minutes! Parallelization does the same by splitting the reading work of a massive library among thousands of computer chips at once.

cards.title: Feeding on the Web
cards.body: To build a smart AI, researchers didn't just give it one encyclopedia. They fed it **BookCorpus** (thousands of digital novels) and the entirety of **Wikipedia**. Thanks to parallel training on thousands of GPUs, the AI finished reading this mountain of text in days rather than centuries, learning how humans write.

cards.title: One Page at a Time?
cards.body:
**Myth:** AI models are trained on large datasets by reading one sentence, understanding it, and then moving to the next.
**Fact:** Transformers process millions of words across thousands of different documents at the exact same millisecond. They are ultimate multitaskers!

cards.title: Sequential vs. Parallel
cards.body: Older models had to read text like a human: one word after another. Transformers break the rules by looking at everything all at once, allowing them to scale to the entire web.

right.text: Processes entire blocks of text at once. Work is easily split across thousands of computer chips. Highly scalable.

left.text: Reads word-by-word. Cannot easily split the work among many computers. Very slow on large datasets.

exercises.prompt: Why do researchers train Transformers on huge datasets like Wikipedia and BookCorpus?

options.text: To provide a massive variety of human language and facts to learn from.
To teach the AI how to code video games from scratch.
Because they are the only websites that do not allow typos.
To make the AI run faster on low-battery phones.

exercises.explain: BookCorpus and Wikipedia provide billions of high-quality sentences that teach the AI grammar, facts, and diverse writing styles.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Parallelization makes it possible to train AI models on web-scale data in a reasonable amount of time.
exercises.explain: Parallelization allows us to divide the training work among thousands of processor chips, shortening the training time from centuries to days.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of Transformer training speed.
exercises.sentence: Transformers scale to huge datasets because they can process data in ________, doing many tasks at the same time.
exercises.explain: Parallel processing allows thousands of computer chips to share the workload, making it fast enough to read huge datasets like Wikipedia.

exercises.prompt: Two of these statements about Transformer scalability are correct. Spot the AI-generated incorrect statement!

options.text: Transformers scale by splitting the training data across thousands of GPUs, processing different sections of web corpora at the same time.
Transformers must read Wikipedia sequentially from the first page to the last page, as parallel processing would mix up the factual order of the encyclopedia.
Because Transformers process all words in a sequence simultaneously, they can easily scale to billions of parameters trained on web-scale datasets.

exercises.explain: Option B is AI-generated nonsense. Transformers process all words at once (parallel), which is why they scale so well to billions of parameters, unlike older models that processed tokens one-by-one.

exercises.prompt: Why did Transformers replace older AI models for training on massive web datasets?

options.text: They require human editors to write out grammar rules before training.
They read sentences one word at a time, making them slow but very careful.
They process all words at once, allowing them to train on massive datasets using many computers.
They are small enough to run on a standard smartphone without internet.

exercises.explain: Transformers became the industry standard because their architecture allows them to process all words in a document simultaneously, meaning we can train them on the entire public internet using thousands of computers working in parallel.
